introSection: Big Dipper was a wooden roller coaster at Geauga Lake & Wildwater Kingdom in Aurora, Ohio, USA.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride opened in 1925 as Sky Rocket.

In the late 1940s the ride was renamed Clipper.

The ride was renamed Big Dipper in 1969.

The ride's fourth drop was originally a double-down drop. It was later modified into a slightly descending straightaway.

The ride underwent a restoration project in 1991. The structure changed from white to a natural wood.

It was the oldest operating roller coaster in Ohio and seventh-oldest in the United States when it closed with the park on September 16, 2007.

In September 2009, following the ride's closure, American Coaster Enthusiasts recognized Big Dipper as an ACE Roller Coaster Landmark. Because the ride never reopened, it was never ceremonially dedicated.

The ride was demolished in 2016.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: 2 trains with 4 cars per train. Riders are arranged 2 across in 3 rows for a total of 24 riders per train. Trains were built by National Amusement Devices and once had working headlights.

fullText:
Big Dipper was a wooden roller coaster at Geauga Lake & Wildwater Kingdom in Aurora, Ohio, USA.

The ride opened in 1925 as Sky Rocket.

In the late 1940s the ride was renamed Clipper.

The ride was renamed Big Dipper in 1969.

The ride's fourth drop was originally a double-down drop. It was later modified into a slightly descending straightaway.

The ride underwent a restoration project in 1991. The structure changed from white to a natural wood.

It was the oldest operating roller coaster in Ohio and seventh-oldest in the United States when it closed with the park on September 16, 2007.

In September 2009, following the ride's closure, American Coaster Enthusiasts recognized Big Dipper as an ACE Roller Coaster Landmark. Because the ride never reopened, it was never ceremonially dedicated.

The ride was demolished in 2016.



2 trains with 4 cars per train. Riders are arranged 2 across in 3 rows for a total of 24 riders per train. Trains were built by National Amusement Devices and once had working headlights.
